hero.headline: How does New Hampshire state income tax work for employers in 2026?
hero.subhead: No withholding. $14,000 UI wage base. Two classification tests most payroll guides miss.
hero.partner_badge: Served by Teamed partner network: Suna Solutions, Inc. (primary US partner)

answer_capsule: New Hampshire has no state income tax on wages as of 2026. The Interest and Dividends Tax — the state's last income-related levy — was fully repealed effective 1st January 2025 under House Bill 2. Employers owe no New Hampshire withholding. UI rates run 0.10%–7.00% on a $14,000 wage base; new employers start at 2.70%. Worker misclassification triggers dual exposure: RSA 281-A:2's 12-criteria test for workers' comp, RSA 282-A:9's ABC test for UI.

income_tax.question: Does New Hampshire have a state income tax on wages?
income_tax.answer_first: No. New Hampshire does not tax wages, salaries, or self-employment income. It never has.
income_tax.body: The state's last income-related tax, the Interest and Dividends Tax at 3%, was repealed effective 1st January 2025. House Bill 2, passed during the 2023 legislative session and signed by Governor Sununu, accelerated the repeal from the originally planned 2026 date. As of today, there is no New Hampshire income tax of any kind. That means no withholding account to register, no state withholding forms to collect, and no state payroll tax calculated from employees' paychecks. Your payroll setup in New Hampshire runs on federal obligations alone: federal income tax (using the employee's W-4), Social Security at 6.2% on the first $184,500, Medicare at 1.45% on all wages. One complexity to keep in mind: employees who split their time between New Hampshire and a state with income tax (Massachusetts is the common case) require withholding on wages earned in the other state. Your EOR handles the allocation.

sources.label: NH DRA: Repeal of I&D Tax Now in Effect
McLane Middleton: I&D Tax Repealed as of January 1

ui_rates.question: What is the New Hampshire unemployment insurance rate in 2026?
ui_rates.answer_first: New employer rate: 2.70% on the first $14,000 per employee. Maximum UI cost per new-employer employee: $378 per year.
ui_rates.body: New Hampshire Employment Security (NHES) sets UI rates annually. Experience-rated employers pay between 0.10% and 7.00% depending on claims history. The taxable wage base is $14,000 per employee per year, one of the lower bases in the US state set. At 2.70%, a new employer's maximum annual UI liability is $378 per employee. At the high end (7.00%), the maximum is $980. NHES mails Tax Rate Determination letters to merit-rated employers by the end of August each year; the rate period runs 1st July to 30th June. State may adjust rates quarterly — watch for notices. File quarterly wage and contribution reports via the NHES WebTax System by the last day of the month following each quarter-end (30th April, 31st July, 31st October, 31st January). File even when no contributions are due. Late filings carry interest plus penalties. New Hampshire has no outstanding federal loan balance, so the full 5.4% FUTA credit applies, reducing effective federal unemployment tax to approximately 0.6% on the first $7,000 per employee.

worker_classification.question: What is the worker classification test in New Hampshire?
worker_classification.answer_first: New Hampshire applies two separate tests. Pass both or the worker is an employee.
worker_classification.body: Most states use a single test. New Hampshire uses two, and passing one does not satisfy the other. For workers' compensation purposes, RSA 281-A:2, VI(b) presumes all workers are employees. To rebut that presumption, an employer must demonstrate that ALL 12 statutory criteria apply. Since 1st January 2011, a signed written agreement affirming all 12 criteria constitutes prima facie evidence — but the agreement alone won't protect you if the working relationship doesn't match it. For unemployment insurance purposes, RSA 282-A:9 applies the ABC Test: the worker must be free from direction and control (Part A), perform services outside the usual course of the employer's business or off any of the employer's premises (Part B), and operate an independently established trade or business (Part C). All three parts must be met. The dual exposure is the practical risk. An employer might satisfy the UI ABC test but fail the 12-criteria workers' comp test because one of the criteria doesn't hold. A misclassification finding on either test carries back-taxes, penalties, and potential workers' comp liability. When in doubt, Teamed's in-house compliance team can run the classification check.

termination.question: How do you terminate an employee in New Hampshire?
termination.answer_first: At-will. No notice period required. Final paycheck due within 72 hours if you terminate.
termination.body: New Hampshire follows at-will employment — either party may end the relationship at any time, for any lawful reason, without advance notice. There is no state statutory notice period for at-will dismissals. The practical obligation is the final paycheck: RSA 275:44 requires payment within 72 hours when the employer terminates. If the employee resigns, the final paycheck is due on the next regular payday. That 72-hour window is tighter than many states, so your payroll provider needs to process the final run immediately on termination notice. Federal protections apply in full: Title VII, the ADA, the ADEA, and the FMLA all govern termination decisions. New Hampshire's own anti-discrimination statute (RSA 354-A) mirrors federal protections and covers employers of six or more. Workers' compensation retaliation claims are a separate risk: RSA 281-A prohibits terminating an employee for filing a workers' comp claim.

workers_comp_wage_hour.question: What are the workers' compensation and wage rules in New Hampshire?
workers_comp_wage_hour.answer_first: Workers' comp is mandatory from employee one. Minimum wage is $7.25, tied to the federal rate.
workers_comp_wage_hour.body: RSA 281-A makes workers' compensation insurance mandatory for all employers with at least one employee. Coverage must come through a private carrier — New Hampshire has no state fund. Report workplace injuries to your carrier within 5 days of notification from the employee. Non-compliance is costly: a $2,500 one-time penalty plus $100 per employee per day, and deliberate non-compliance is a class B felony. On wages: New Hampshire's minimum wage is set by RSA 279:21 at the federal floor — currently $7.25 per hour, unchanged since 2009. There is no scheduled increase. Tipped employees may be paid $3.27 per hour cash wage with a $3.98 tip credit, provided total compensation reaches $7.25. Teamed handles new-hire reporting (Form NHES 0085), quarterly NHES WebTax filings, workers' comp coordination with Suna Solutions, and final paycheck processing. You get a named Teamed operations contact, not a portal and a chatbot.

good_to_know.question: What should you know before hiring in New Hampshire?
good_to_know.body: New Hampshire is the only US state with no income tax of any kind as of 2025. That makes payroll setup genuinely simpler — no state withholding account, no state tax form collection, no quarterly state withholding filings. The compliance surface is narrower than most states. Where complexity lives is on two fronts: worker classification and multi-state workers. The dual IC test (RSA 281-A:2 for workers' comp, RSA 282-A:9 for UI) catches contractors who pass one test but fail the other. And the Boston metro draws many workers who live in New Hampshire but commute to Massachusetts — that split creates a Massachusetts withholding obligation on the Massachusetts wages even when the employer account is NH-based.

faq.q: Does New Hampshire have a state income tax in 2026?
faq.a: No. New Hampshire does not tax wages, salaries, or any earned income. The Interest and Dividends Tax, the state's last income levy, was fully repealed effective 1st January 2025 under House Bill 2. No employer withholding is required.

faq.q: What is the New Hampshire unemployment insurance rate for new employers in 2026?
faq.a: New employers in New Hampshire pay UI at 2.70% on the first $14,000 of each employee's wages per year, for a maximum annual cost of $378 per employee. Experience-rated employers pay between 0.10% and 7.00%.

faq.q: What is the worker classification test in New Hampshire?
faq.a: New Hampshire uses two tests. Workers' compensation uses the 12-criteria test under RSA 281-A:2 — all 12 must be met. Unemployment insurance uses the ABC test under RSA 282-A:9 — all three parts must be met. Passing one test does not satisfy the other.

faq.q: What is the New Hampshire final paycheck law?
faq.a: Under RSA 275:44, if an employer terminates an employee, the final paycheck is due within 72 hours. If the employee resigns, payment is due on the next regular payday.

faq.q: What is the minimum wage in New Hampshire in 2026?
faq.a: New Hampshire's minimum wage equals the federal minimum wage: $7.25 per hour. RSA 279:21 ties the state rate to the federal floor. It has not increased since 2009.

meta.title: New Hampshire employer taxes 2026: no income tax, UI rates, classification
meta.description: NH has no state income tax on wages — I&D Tax repealed 1 Jan 2025. New employer UI rate 2.70% on $14,000 wage base. Dual IC test under RSA 281-A:2 and RSA 282-A:9 explained.
